ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for yam-filled bread rolls; closest defensible anchor is the generic bread roll food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for filled sweet yam buns; A000E (Bread and rolls) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for yam-filled bread bun; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard yam bun weighs approximately 80 g, which is a typical single-serving portion as sold at Singaporean bakeries and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Yam Bun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yam Bun (cr_id F05020145), confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bread Products — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on sweet filled bread roll
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singaporean yam bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: sweet filled bread rolls with enriched wheat flour base, which cluster in the GI 50–58 range in the Sydney database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 36.19) / 100 ≈ 19.6, using an 80 g serving providing 36.19 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18.78 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.91 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium is also moderate at 209 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit yam bun consumption because the high sugar load (18.78 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (1.91 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding the direct sodium contribution of 209 mg/100 g and elevating cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 18.78 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g threshold; dietary fibre only 1.91 g/100 g provides insufficient buffering, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid yam bun or limit to a very small occasional portion, as the high sugar content (18.78 g/100 g) combined with low fibre (1.91 g/100 g) produces a rapid and significant postprandial glucose spike; if consumed, pair with a high-fibre food to partially blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.2 g/100 g from shortening; high refined sugar load (18.78 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial stress
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit yam bun intake because the combination of saturated fat from shortening (approximately 4.2 g/100 g), high refined sugar (18.78 g/100 g), and moderate sodium (209 mg/100 g) collectively elevates LDL cholesterol, promotes endothelial inflammation, and increases blood pressure risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yam Bun (芋头包, Yu Tou Bao) is a soft, pillowy bread roll filled with a sweetened yam (taro) paste, widely sold at Singaporean bakeries, coffee shops, and hawker stalls. The outer dough is made from enriched wheat flour, yeast, sugar, eggs, milk, and shortening, producing a light, slightly sweet crumb. The filling is a smooth, violet-tinged paste made from cooked yam blended with sugar and fat, delivering a mildly earthy, sweet flavour with a creamy texture. Each bun typically weighs 70–90 g and is consumed as a breakfast item or afternoon snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 317 kcal, 6.58 g protein, 12.1 g fat, 45.24 g carbohydrate, 18.78 g sugar, 1.91 g dietary fibre, and 209 mg sodium. The high sugar content relative to its low fibre level makes this food unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The shortening used in both the dough and filling contributes saturated fat, raising cardiovascular concern. Despite a moderate GI of approximately 54, the glycaemic load per standard serving is approximately 19.6, which is clinically significant. Clinicians and dietitians should counsel patients with metabolic conditions to avoid or strictly limit this item.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a yam bun?
Is yam bun suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. The food is a canonical Singaporean bakery item with a well-established HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from typical shortening-based bread roll composition using USDA SR Legacy data as a secondary reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item and are not available in any other approved source for this specific preparation. Saturated_fat_g (4.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable shortening-enriched sweet bread rolls (USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 174924, 'Bread, sweet roll, commercially prepared') and should be treated as an approximation. Trans fat is set to -1 as the HPB record does not report it; however, the presence of shortening in the formulation means trace trans fat cannot be excluded. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on sweet filled bread rolls; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean yam bun was found in any approved source. Glycaemic load per serving = (54 × 36.19) / 100 ≈ 19.6, based on an 80 g serving providing 36.19 g carbohydrate. All core macros (protein_g 6.58, fat_g 12.1, carbohydrate_g 45.24) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
